speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three with Mrs. Wanda Mehr. We're talking about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? I'm ?] Mrs. Wanda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wanda. We're talking about your family home, when you were growing up. What was the home language used at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, people spoke Yiddish. In my place, my mother spoke often Polish with the children. Because the children belonged to organ-- Zionistic or-- my mother was a Zionist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father-- as I said, Labor Zionist. I wrote it down there. And [? Hashomer Hatzair ?] [INAUDIBLE]. I remember I was only six years old when my daughter-- because I could sing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my eldest-- eldest sister was [? Hashomer Hatzair ?]. And she took me along. She grabbed me by my hand, took me with her. And people made me sing there-- a little girl of six singing Hebrew songs. I didn't know the Hebrew language then. I don't know if I didn't mix it up, but I sang in, [INAUDIBLE]. It's a Hebrew song. I remember all the songs now. And there, people didn't know much Hebrew then. But like, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] means the older one in spoke [? among ?] in Hebrew. And I listened. I was always keen to listen to languages. And I listened. And I picked up a few Hebrew words. And he-- then my sister enrolled me in [? Hashomer Hatzair ?]. Since I was six, I actually grew up in [INAUDIBLE]. And that was Labor Zionist. And before-- because we-- mostly students belonged there. In the city, there a lot of Jewish students. We formed [? Hashomer ?]. I was the youngest. They formed [? Hashomer Hatzair ?]. And there, people, when they gathered there, only a few knew Hebrew. They learned it at the [NON-ENGLISH]. There was a [NON-ENGLISH] in [INAUDIBLE]. I remember exactly where the [NON-ENGLISH] was. They learned in [NON-ENGLISH] Hebrew, but only a few people knew Hebrew. So they spoke Polish. Because mostly, they were students. The whole [? Hashomer Hatzair ?], it's different now here. They were mostly started from students. And they spoke Polish. And we came home. We spoke Polish. And my mother, she knew perfect Polish. So she spoke with us, to the people, Polish, not Yiddish. We didn't speak much Yiddish at all. It was my father, yes, from time to time. But we didn't think of it. We spoke whatever came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father speak Polish as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, he had to as a merchant mingling with people. But his Polish was not as good as my mother's. I don't think he spoke very grammatical Polish, but he spoke Polish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: He understood Ukrainian, too, because he mixed with those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at home, it was mostly Polish and some Yiddish that was spoken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish-- sometimes, my mother wanted me to learn German because part of her family lived in Nuremberg in Germany. And they later settled in Israel. But they're not alive, only their children-- means cousins are still in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother wanted me to learn German. That's why I picked up-- I studied in high school German. Primaries, there was no German. There was Ukrainian only-- Polish and Ukrainian in primary. So I learned German as well and I later learned Hebrew a little, not much. My Hebrew-- I don't think my Hebrew is perfect, but I do speak Hebrew. The other Jewish children your age-- did they speak Polish as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some-- some, like the doctor's children or the chemist's children, some. But mostly, they didn't speak, especially boys who didn't attend a high school. Only [NON-ENGLISH], only primary, and then the [NON-ENGLISH]. They didn't speak. Even now, I often meet people from Poland. I met some. And they don't speak good Polish at all. But they are saying-- they cover it up that I forgot. I forgot the language. You never forget a language. If you speak the language well, you never forget it. You can forget perhaps Hebrew because we don't use in every day Hebrew here. You can forget a little, but it comes back.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mention--

speaker: interviewee
text: So the people, the Jews, didn't speak a-- a good Polish, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that you had Ukrainian in your first public school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any opportunity to use Ukrainian outside the school at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because even Ukrainian children spoke Polish. It was Poland. We had a neighbor, Ukrainian. He was a Ukrainian but in the Polish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Though it was his children sometimes-- we were in the same school. Sometimes, because at home with their father they speak Ukrainian, they spoke Ukrainian. So I knew the language. But otherwise, no, mostly Polish. The Ukrainians among themselves speak Ukrainian, but not at large, you know? You mentioned a little bit earlier about the Tarbut. What was the Tarbut?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tarbut was Zionistic. I don't remember that was it Labor or not. But I know it was Zionistic organization. Tarbut means, in Polish, [NON-ENGLISH]. And they used to-- they were older people, not young people, because young people were [? Hashomer Hatzair ?]. Or there was-- there were other organizations, more rightist. This was leftist. They have them here too. There were a few others. And Tarbut had a school. Not a school-- there were classes where they were in the [? hall. ?] And they taught Hebrew there. They wanted people to learn Hebrew. But they were older people, up to 40, even-- 35, 40, 30, mostly older people and mostly men. I don't know why not women. And there were lectures. People used to go to lectures. Grodek was considered a very small, intellectual place. Because not in all other small places there were so many organizations-- quite a number of organizations-- in that Tarbut itself. And people [BACKGROUND NOISE] learned Hebrew-- not the parents, but the children, yes, my age or older. But they were mostly very young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the other organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: More rightist, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never thought of it. But you have some here-- Jabotinsky's, you know. That was more rightist, not leftist. [? Ze'ev ?] Jabotinsky. And I don't remember exactly, but there-- I know there were rightist organizations. I mean, you have them here. I never thought of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in general--

speaker: interviewee
text: In general, it was a very pleasant Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] in [NON-ENGLISH]. It was considered an Grodek, you know. Because they lived together and participated in all-- there were, for instance, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when I came up to remember Jabotinsky, his death, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So it-- they made evenings, special evenings. And everybody went there. Though we met together with each other, you know? The people met there. At that time in the late '20s, [BACKGROUND NOISE] early '30s, there was some-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was later-- later than-- later-- '20s, [BACKGROUND NOISE] yes. Very late '20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, some-- some people in the Jewish community actually believed in going to Israel, didn't they? Palestine--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember much of them, the older people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the younger people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember my sister, who was born in 8. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she was about-- she didn't even finish her schooling. That was a trend of students to leave even high school, not speaking of higher schools, to leave in-- in groups. They were the very first-- I forgot how they called them. From Russia, the first people who arrived in Israel were [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know if you know about it. I remember. I was told it later on. But she was much older than me. She was the oldest sister and was about the [INAUDIBLE] after her. I said when I was six, she grabbed me and took me with her by hand and took me with her-- six or even less. But she belonged to an older group, to those called-- here, they call them [NON-ENGLISH]. And she spoke Hebrew. She learned it in the Tarbut. And I remember exactly doctor's daughter and two sons. They were at university both. They left university. Oh, it was-- they took pride in-- to leave schools and go to Israel. And a whole group-- my sister was in it. There were older ones and she was-- there were some younger ones-- a group enrolling to go to Israel. Illegal-- that was illegal then. It was so many years back before independence of Israel. And I don't remember the year when it was exactly. I was young, and I wasn't so concerned. I remember only she took me by my hand when I was little. And she said, I'm going away. I couldn't even understand exactly what she wanted at six. I'm going to Israel. Don't tell mother. She wouldn't let her go. Parents didn't let go. Don't tell. Till I'll be on my way there, don't tell. When you go home-- it was in the afternoon. And she didn't go home. She slept at her friend's place. They wouldn't let her go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And said, don't tell anybody. You can tell them tomorrow when I'll be far away that I went to Israel. I went home, and I cried. I cried and cried. My mother said, why do you-- what happened to you? Anything-- anyone hurt you? No. What-- why do you cry? Because-- because-- because Sala-- we called her not Sara, but Sala. Because Sala will not come back. What do you mean? Where from? She told me not to tell you. So when will you tell me? Tomorrow. I didn't tell. I waited until tomorrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother couldn't understand why she didn't come home. Next day, I couldn't sleep all night. It was still dark, went up, went to my mother. I said, she went to Israel. Oh-- later, she said, maybe it's for better. But other parents-- [INAUDIBLE] went to Israel and got back [INAUDIBLE] daughter or their son, got them back. But later on, they went back again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's why-- how my sister. But I remember when we all children stood at the platform at the station as the train came. I don't know how they went.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was illegal. I don't remember exactly. It was arranged through higher and through the [NON-ENGLISH] or whatever. I don't know. We stood on the platform. And suddenly, despite that they were very anti-Semitic, the Poles, on the platform, it was different in here. It was a very big-- Here, you have the benches all over-- a very big platform at the station. And they danced a hora. All those people-- I never forget it. They danced, and they grabbed me into it. I was little tiny, like this. They grabbed me into it. Suddenly, I learned the hora. I think I have a painting somewhere, the hora from that. And they all danced the hora. I even remember the song, that we will see each other-- in Hebrew. We will see each other in the near future in Israel. We don't-- we won't leave anyone here. You come to [INAUDIBLE]. It was [NON-ENGLISH], I think. [NON-ENGLISH] is working people. The working Israel needs you. Come with us. It's a song I remember. And then, when she left for Israel, and I told you the story when she came back and she was finally with her husband, with her child, killed-- she married in Israel later. She was in a kibbutz. I just want to remember the kibbutz. I knew-- I visited that kibbutz later on. There were some people, her friends there. But I think they're not anymore there. Some died there in the kibbutz. I can't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister come back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she did, but many years later before the German-- before the Second World War, before '39. She came back because her husband came from Lwow. And they had-- oh, that's a whole story.

speaker: interviewer
text: I lived later on in Lwow, and I got married not far from her. Her-- his grandmother passed away and there was a [NON-ENGLISH] left. [NON-ENGLISH] is in Hebrew. You know what's [NON-ENGLISH]? Testament.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she left-- there were quite wealthy people in Lwow in Poland-- left a big, big house, a building, and a restaurant in front. There was a big restaurant belonging to that grandmother. She was a widow. And she died at ripe age. And she left it all to her grandchildren. Because her daughter was in that too. She was widowed too. She lived [? hence ?] at the restaurant. And it had to be the house divided among-- she had three grandchildren, three boys, with children. And that building had to be divided, and he had to be then there. So he came back from Israel just to return later on to Israel. It was a kibbutz. I don't remember now. I know-- I knew it. I would call it later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came there and my daughter, my-- sorry. My sister was only a short time married there to him. His name was Erb. That's why I put down that [INAUDIBLE] my sister's name later was from Spiegler changed to Erb.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as she came back, she got her [? part, ?] a big flat, furniture-- beautiful flat and furniture on [INAUDIBLE] in Lwow, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And she got pregnant, though my mother wanted her to stay and have the child in Poland where she lived. It was a beautiful place there. And wait until-- so she had to wait nearly a year. And so the child was very young. And she waited again. They were not-- not in a hurry to return to Israel because they could return any time later on. So it was before independence. But since they were citizens of Israel-- they had documents-- they stayed so long until the boy was five years old. They lived a very comfortable life. And there in my brother-in-law's-- his brother got a wife too with no children, got another flat. It was a huge place with a restaurant. The restaurant was left to his mother and some younger children. And the three elder children were-- got flats. I don't know what they did there, their professions. And finally, '39 started the war. They were trapped. They couldn't go back. So my-- I think I mentioned to you that my brother-in-law, my sister's husband, he studied once, how do you call, languages. He knew a number of languages, including Italian and others-- Greek and other languages. He knew quite a lot of languages. When the Germans came, the Italians-- Mussolini, you know, went together with the Germans. They-- I don't know how many were [INAUDIBLE] of them, but a number of, I don't know, divisions or how you call them, Italians, came to Lwow, worked hand in hand with the Nazis. You know that from the history. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were-- they were part of Poland where they stood there. One side was Park [INAUDIBLE] it was called, I remember. It was a huge park. And there were buildings-- or they build them especially for the German troops. The other side nearby were the Italians. I don't know how he got them because I told you before that they took people to work there. It was not known then.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't know about the concentration camps. It was in the beginning. So somehow, my brother-in-law got employed by the Italians because he knew Italian. And they kept him in their [INAUDIBLE]. How do you call the [INAUDIBLE]? Camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a camp it was, yes. They kept him. He told them-- they were not anti-Semitic. Or-- so they went with the Nazis but they were not-- the people, the army, was not-- the Italian was not anti-Semitic. He told them-- they knew that he's Jewish. He told-- they liked him because he understood their language and others and German as well. And he told-- told them that he has a wife and a child. He can't leave them. They were already in the ghetto then. It was the beginning of the ghetto. It was then when I was there for two weeks in the ghetto only. He told them that if they could help him to hide somewhere in their camp. They said, yes, bring it over. Next day when he went to-- but he can't-- he didn't stay there overnight. He came back to the ghetto. And the next morning, he had to come-- go back to work, to them. Children were not allowed out of the camp, of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told he was a tall man, a big, tall man, with a big coat. It was I think late autumn or early winter. He took the child of five year, a little boy, under his coat, tied him around to his waist, covered him with his coat. That's how he marched up to them, up to there, to the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] where the camp was, both Nazi and Italians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they hid there-- she didn't have to be-- there she could be hidden because he was-- they were afraid maybe some of Italians would give her out. So they kept them in a house somewhere with the child and fed them. But a few months later, it is known that there was a rift between the Italians and Nazis, between Mussolini and Hitler. I don't know exactly the history, but it's known there was a rift between them. And the Italians-- the army had to go back to Italy, leave Germany-- actually, Poland. But the German-- it was occupied by the Germans. And as they had to go back, my brother-in-law and my sister and my-- their child didn't know where to go. Then the Germans took over and took him away. They didn't need a Jew-- a translator. They had theirs. Not [INAUDIBLE] didn't know. They didn't want a Jew. So he probably, because we never heard of him, killed him. And my sister understood. It never came back that they killed him. She had no place where to go with the child. So she went out on the street. I don't think she was confused. She knew what she wants to-- she didn't want to live anymore, not only because she had nowhere to go. I was then with a Polish woman and my child. That Polish woman was not far there. She has seen her. She gave me the last-- she told me the last words what she said. She said she didn't know my [INAUDIBLE] name. She said, [? Eda ?] is happy. She's lucky she's with you. And she has seen it. She told me that she has seen it. [INAUDIBLE] She went out and on the street to the first gestapo man who patrolled the streets. She went to him and said, you took my husband. You forgot me and my child. Gave herself up with the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year do you think this was in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at the beginning. I think it was all in '42. I think the beginning [INAUDIBLE] ghetto started there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you go quickly back to your time in Grodek, as you mentioned, some people like your sister wanted to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the same sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right. What were some of the other philosophies of that time in the Jewish groups? Did they have differences?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean-- during the-- in the Nazi occupation?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, in the '30s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The younger people were all Zionistic. There were some Hasidic. We didn't mix with them, not like here.

speaker: interviewer
text: We knew who they are, but they were Hasidic. They didn't mix, and they never went together with the Zionists. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was even an antipathy, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't like each others-- here too. The [INAUDIBLE] don't go to Israel. [BACKGROUND NOISE] So we were not concerned about the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any philosophy in the Jewish community about assimilating with the Polish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was a case. He was a professor, our professor in gymnasium, Professor [? Taub. ?] I remember-- still remember him. Professor is-- Some people are here. I don't think-- I never met anyone who was in Grodek. He was later professor of university in Lwow, Professor [? Taub. ?] He had a son and a daughter. They were-- and he survived. They used to go out and play tennis, but they didn't consider-- I mean, they were Jews. But if they would be open Jews, he wouldn't be a professor at the gymnasium. They were considered rather by Jewish people. They never mixed with Jews. They were not-- they were Poles, of course-- Poles, citizens. But they were-- we knew that they were Jews. But people didn't like him, the Jewish people, because they never mixed with Jewish people. His daughter, Professor [? Taub's, ?] his daughter married a non-Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were rather assimilated. His daughter married a non-- but I tell you the story of his daughter who married a non-Jew, a doctor, a non-Jew. They went to live from [INAUDIBLE] to Lwow and had a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she had a child, a baby, during the Nazi occupation. And some people told me they saw her on the street. It means that the husband-- doctor, Polish-- left her during the Nazi occupation. And she had to go with the child to the ghetto back. She was Jewish. [BACKGROUND NOISE] She couldn't-- even she was very much assimilated. But she was still considered Jewish by the Germans. She had to go to the ghetto with a baby. I don't think she lives anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just asked you about the living conditions in Grodek. How many people were living in your family in your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many people? [BACKGROUND NOISE] Three families-- no, four families. There was a-- it wasn't on the-- we were on the floor, first floor. And downstairs at the front, there was-- it wasn't a restaurant, and it was not our house. It was an apartment. So, the owner of the house, he was considered a wealthy man. But he was religious. He lived at the front. It wasn't a restaurant-- I think like a winery. He used to sell only-- not only sell. People used to go into his place and drink, sit there and drink. It was-- in front was that place where people used to drink alcoholic beverages or anything like it. And at the back was his apartment. He had two sons. One was a Hassid, the younger one. And that younger one was, I remember as a child, I remember him. He was much older. Could be used to be like my sister's age, eldest. The other one was a Zionist, went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. We'll continue the story the next tape.
